coredata.dc:description: The advancement of sophisticated photocatalytic materials for environmental amelioration has become increasingly imperative in the context of persistent organic pollutants in aqueous effluents. The amalgamation of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with nanocomposite constituents has demonstrated promising prospects in augmenting photocatalytic efficiency. Nevertheless, obstacles persist in the realms of optimizing photon absorption and the segregation of electron-hole pairs. The application of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within this sphere, particularly in synergy with amino-functionalized MOFs such as UiO-66-NH2, remains underexplored. In this study, we elucidate the synthesis of UiO-66@Polysulfone porous microspheres, augmented by CQDs, employing an innovative Pickering emulsion template approach for the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline. Our findings reveal that this composite material not only manifests a distinctive hollow microsphere architecture, contributing to an elevated specific surface area, but also markedly enhances the degradation efficacies of both methylene blue and tetracycline under visible light irradiation. Moreover, this research accentuates the material's exceptional recyclability and stability, highlighting its potential as a sustainable and efficacious solution for the remediation of wastewater and organic pollutant degradation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The advancement of sophisticated photocatalytic materials for environmental amelioration has become increasingly imperative in the context of persistent organic pollutants in aqueous effluents. The amalgamation of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with nanocomposite constituents has demonstrated promising prospects in augmenting photocatalytic efficiency. Nevertheless, obstacles persist in the realms of optimizing photon absorption and the segregation of electron-hole pairs. The application of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within this sphere, particularly in synergy with amino-functionalized MOFs such as UiO-66-NH2, remains underexplored. In this study, we elucidate the synthesis of UiO-66@Polysulfone porous microspheres, augmented by CQDs, employing an innovative Pickering emulsion template approach for the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline. Our findings reveal that this composite material not only manifests a distinctive hollow microsphere architecture, contributing to an elevated specific surface area, but also markedly enhances the degradation efficacies of both methylene blue and tetracycline under visible light irradiation. Moreover, this research accentuates the material's exceptional recyclability and stability, highlighting its potential as a sustainable and efficacious solution for the remediation of wastewater and organic pollutant degradation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the epoch of industrialization, aquatic contamination has emerged as a formidable global quandary, presenting considerable impediments to the sustenance of human habitats [1–4]. The widespread utilization of organic pollutants, notably methylene blue and tetracyclines encompassing organic dyes and antibiotics, has precipitated an escalation in their prevalence within aquatic systems, thereby inflicting substantial detriment to these environments [5–7]. Characterized by exogenous bases and intricate aromatic configurations, these organic dyes and antibiotics exhibit pronounced stability under photonic interaction, rendering them recalcitrant to degradation and necessitating oxygenative digestion [8–10]. Previous scholarly investigations have elucidated that conventional aqueous treatment methodologies, including adsorption [11], membrane filtration [12], chemical flocculation [13], ion exchange [14], microwave catalysis [15], encounter inherent constraints in the extirpation of these organic contaminants [16–19]. Consequently, the imperative to develop efficacious, eco-benign, and sustainable technologies for water treatment has attained paramount importance.

Photocatalytic technology, heralded as a water treatment methodology of considerable promise, has garnered escalating scholarly attention in recent epochs. This technology stands distinguished from traditional water purification strategies by virtue of its rapid reaction kinetics, obviating the necessity for chemical reagent supplementation and circumventing the generation of secondary effluents [20–23]. Concurrently, to amplify the efficacy of photocatalytic processes, there has been an unceasing quest among researchers to innovate and incorporate novel photocatalytic substances, thereby bolstering their photoreactive capabilities. Within this context, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have captivated considerable interest, attributed to their prodigious specific surface areas, copious active loci, and modifiable pore architecture [24–26]. UiO-66-NH2, an amino-functionalized MOF, not only demonstrates commendable stability but also furnishes an optimal adsorptive milieu for organic pollutants, courtesy of its porous structure [27]. As a photocatalyst, it is capable of generating reactive oxygen species (ROS). Nonetheless, its catalytic activity is hampered by the paucity of active sites, the precipitous recombination of photogenerated charges, and suboptimal absorption of visible light [28–32].

The carbonaceous and hydrophilic attributes of carbon quantum dots (CQD) significantly augment photocatalytic proficiency through the absorption of visible light and the inhibition of recombination in photo-generated electron/hole pairs [33–35]. Xiang et al. have elucidated a novel methodology, a one-step double solvents approach, for concurrently integrating dual functional entities within the pores of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), thereby enhancing the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of the host photocatalyst [35]. A pioneering composite photocatalyst, carbon quantum dots modified potassium titanate nanotubes (CQDs/K2Ti6O13), was synthesized through an amalgamation of hydrothermal treatment and subsequent calcination. Chen et al. propose a viable technique for the modification of titanate nanomaterials, positing the developed CQDs/K2Ti6O13 hybrid as a promising candidate for the remediation of antibiotics in aquatic environments [36]. A ternary heterostructure photocatalyst, synthesized via the co-anchoring of graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) on BiVO4 microspheres, exhibits exceptional photocatalytic activity for the degradation of rhodamine B and tetracycline (TC) under visible-light exposure [37]. Furthermore, a novel urea-modified carbon quantum dots (NCDs) decorated g-C3N4/WO3 Z-scheme heterojunction was prepared through a combined approach of in situ calcination and physical deposition [38]. Analytical experimentation on radical capture revealed that superoxide ion radicals (·O2 –) are the primary active species in photocatalytic degradation, leading to a proposed degradation mechanism for g-C3N4/WO3/NCDs. Various elements, including noble and transition metals, non-metals, and metalloids (e.g., graphene, carbon nanotube, carbon quantum dots), are doped into photocatalysts as co-catalysts to amplify photodegradation performance. Koe has meticulously delineated diverse categories of heterogeneous photocatalysts, encompassing mechanisms, synthesis methods of biomass-supported photocatalysts, photocatalytic degradation of organic substances in authentic wastewater, along with designs of photocatalytic reactors and their operational parameters, in addition to the latest advancements in photocatalyst-incorporated membranes [39]. The composites of MOFs amalgamated with quantum dots (QDs) showcase elevated photocatalytic capabilities. The synthesis methodologies, structures, and applications of QD-MOF composites in various domains such as water splitting for hydrogen production, carbon dioxide (CO2) reduction to methane (CH4), degradation of organic dyes, reduction of Cr(VI), and oxidation of nitric oxide (NO) are comprehensively discussed [40]. Nonetheless, the potential of CQDs and MOFs as highly efficient, recyclable composite photocatalysts has not been fully explored and documented in existing literature.

In this study, we commenced by covalently linking the amino moieties of UiO-66-NH2 with the carboxyl groups of CQDs, thus effectuating the functionalization of UiO-66-NH2 with CQD. Subsequently, through the application of the Pickering emulsion template methodology, we engendered an emulsion of polyurethane, stabilized by CQD@UiO-66-NH2, thereby fabricating a novel genre of composite porous hollow microspheres, sheathed and steadied by CQD-modified MOF composite nanoparticles (Fig. 1 a). This innovative composite hollow microsphere not only boasts a high specific surface area and a plethora of active sites inherent to UiO-66-NH2 but also exhibits the eminent photocatalytic proficiency and the advantageous attributes of low-density recyclability characteristic of CQDs. We conducted a comprehensive examination of the photocatalytic degradation efficacy of this composite material upon methylene blue and tetracycline, and delved into the pivotal factors influencing degradation efficiency. This investigation furnishes both theoretical and experimental underpinnings for the development of new, efficient photocatalysts to realize the efficacious degradation of organic pollutants. This study, therefore, not only expands the purview of MOFs in the realm of wastewater purification and treatment but also contributes novel insights into the quest for highly efficient photocatalytic materials.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine, polysulfone, dichloromethane (DCM, ≥ 99.5 %), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP, ≥ 99.5 %), methylene blue, Zirconium chloride (ZrCl4, 97 %), anhydrous ethanol (EtOH), amino-terephthalic acid (NH2-BDC, 98 %), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 98 %), N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N'-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were all purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). The hydrogen peroxide solution of concentration at about 30 % was obtained from Chinese Medicine Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (FLEX4, Elga PURELAB, UK) with a resistivity of 18.2 M Ω·cm. Other reagents are analytical grade without further treatment.

The synthesis of UiO-66-NH2 is executed via a solvent-thermal method [41,42]. Initially, 0.6438 g of ZrCl4 and 0.5108 g of NH2-BDC are combined in 50 mL DMF, followed by a 15-minute sonication. Subsequently, 10 mL glacial acetic acid is incorporated, and the mixture is stirred continuously for 2 h. This blend is then transferred to a high-pressure reaction kettle and subjected to a reaction at 130 °C for 24 h. Post-reaction, the product is sequentially washed with DMF and anhydrous ethanol, and subsequently dried at 100 °C for 12 h. For CQD synthesis, 5 g of citric acid and 2.5 g ethylenediamine are dissolved in 50 mL deionized water within a round-bottom flask. The solution is then heated to 160 °C and maintained at this temperature under consistent stirring for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution undergoes dialysis against deionized water for 48 h using a 1 K MWCO dialysis bag, with bi-hourly water replacements. The dialysate is collected and freeze-dried to procure amine-functionalized CQDs. In the final step, 0.2 wt% (relative to water) CQD is introduced into a 0.1 wt% (relative to water) UiO-66-NH2 aqueous solution and magnetically stirred for 24 h in the presence of EDC/NHS (0.1 and 0.3 wt% respectively, relative to UiO-66-NH2) to facilitate the binding of UiO-66-NH2 with CQD. To eliminate excess CQD and EDC/NHS, the solution is centrifuged multiple times at 8,000 rpm with deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CUP porous microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of CQD@UiO-66-NH2/PSF (CUP) porous microspheres utilizes the Pickering emulsion evaporation method. Initially, a homogenous solution is created by dissolving 0.125 g of PSF and 0.1 g of PVP in 6.0 mL of NMP/DCM (1/5 vol ratio). Subsequently, varying amounts of CQD@UiO-66-NH2, ranging from 0.01 to 0.11 g, are added to this solution. The mixture is stirred for 30 min to ensure complete dissolution of all components. This solution is then gradually introduced into a 50 mL coagulation bath containing a 1 % PVA aqueous solution through a microchannel, leading to the formation of CUP composite hollow microspheres. The mixture is left to stir at room temperature for 5 h. The CUP microspheres are then filtered using a 500-mesh sieve, washed sequentially with ethanol and distilled water, and re-filtered to remove any loosely adhered CQD@UiO-66-NH2 particles from the microsphere surfaces. The final product is dried at 60℃ and designated as CUP-x, where x denotes the mass ratio between the suspension liquid's CQD@UiO-66-NH2 and PSF, varying from 0.08 to 0.88. For comparative purposes, PSF microbeads without CQD@UiO-66-NH2 and those with only UIO-66-NH2 are also synthesized under similar conditions.

The structural and compositional analysis of CUP microspheres involves multiple sophisticated techniques. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), using a Hitachi Regulus 8220, is employed to elucidate the surface morphology. For cross-sectional examination, the material is finely ground using a ball mill. The fluorescent properties of CUP-x microspheres are assessed with a confocal laser scanning microscope (SP8-X, Leica, Germany). Prior to this analysis, 5 mg of the microspheres are uniformly dispersed in 8 mL of water, with 1 mL of this suspension placed on a microscope slide, covered by a glass coverslip. The excitation and emission spectra of CUP-x peak at 443.6 nm and 539 nm, respectively. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) is utilized to determine the chemical elements present on the surface of the CUP microspheres. The crystalline structure is explored through a D8-Advance X-ray diffraction spectrometer (Bruker, Germany), with the X-ray diffraction spectrum recorded over a range of 5° to 90°. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Vertex-V70, Bruker, Germany) is conducted to analyze the functional group content in CUP. This process involves three independent tests for each sample to ensure reliability, with results averaged for accuracy. The porosity of the CUP microspheres is quantified using a weight method, with the specific calculation formula provided in the subsequent section.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Scientific, USA) revealed the chemical composition of XUP. Optical properties of CUP were studied by a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Lambda 950, PerkinElmer Instrument, USA) and Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer. The photoelectrochemical performance of CUP was measured using an electrochemical workstation (CS310, China). Mercury intrusion porosimetry is the preferred method for characterizing the large pores, or interparticle pores, of materials, as the nitrogen adsorption technique is insufficient for this purpose. Additionally, this method is essential for determining the volume of saturated imbibition during fluid imbibition processes. The structural parameters of the microsphere CUP-x are accurately measured using a Micromeritic 9620 Mercury Intrusion Porosimeter (Micromeritics, USA). Prior to measurement, it is crucial to eliminate moisture and water vapor from the samples. This is achieved by drying them in an oven at 105 °C for a minimum duration of 24 h. The samples prepared for analysis typically weigh around 0.7 g (equivalent to 0.3 cm3). The mercury intrusion porosimeter operates under a maximum pressure of 414 MPa, capable of detecting pores with a minimum diameter of 5 nm.

To assess the adsorption efficacy of CUP microspheres for Methylene Blue (MB), a methodical experimental approach is employed. A quantity of 50 mg of CUP microspheres is immersed in 20 mL of a 10 mg/L MB solution. This mixture is then stirred continuously in a dark environment at a temperature of 25 °C for a duration of 30 min. To monitor the adsorption process, the solution is sampled at five-minute intervals, and the MB concentration is quantitatively analyzed using an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer. The instantaneous adsorption capacity (qt) of the CUP microspheres is calculated based on the observed changes in the MB concentration. The specific calculation formula employed for determining qt is detailed in the following section.

where, Cr0 (g/L) and Ct (g/L) represent the initial concentration of MB and the concentration at time t respectively, V (L) and m(g) represent the volume of dye solution and mass of adsorbent. For comparison, 50 mg UiO-66-NH2, 50 mg CQD@UiO-66-NH2 and 50 mg PSF microspheres were used in adsorption experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of CUP porous microcapsules
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic degradation capabilities of CUP, UiO-66-NH2, and PSF towards MB and TC are evaluated using a XPA-7 photocatalytic device (Nanjing Xujiang Electromechanical Factory, China). The experiments are conducted under ambient, open-air conditions at room temperature. A 350 W xenon lamp, positioned 8 cm from the reflector light source, serves as the irradiation source for the Fenton MB degradation system. The UV filter employed in this setup has a cut-off wavelength of 420 nm. Prior to initiating the reaction, 50 mg of the chosen catalyst is immersed in 20 mL of a 10 mg/L MB solution. This mixture is then agitated vigorously in the dark for 30 min to ensure the attainment of adsorption equilibrium. Subsequently, the suspension is subjected to ultraviolet light emanating from the 350 W xenon lamp, initiating the photocatalytic degradation process. Sampling is conducted at 10-minute intervals, wherein 1.0 mL of the reaction liquid is collected. Post-centrifugation, the degradation efficiency is quantified using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The degradation rate of MB is calculated using equation (4), which is detailed in the subsequent section.
(4)

where, Cr0 and C are the dye concentrations at irradiation time t = 0 and t respectively. Then, the obtained UiO-66-NH2/PSF and CUP (CQD content is 0.2 wt%, 0.5 wt% and 1.0 wt%, relative to UiO-66-NH2) were used as photocatalysts for photocatalytic degradation of TC in a 100 mL tetracycline aqueous solution (10 mg/L) under simulated sunlight. The ultraviolet–visible light comes from a 300 W xenon lamp equipped with an infrared cutoff filter (λ > 800 nm). Before irradiation, the solution was continuously stirred in darkness for 20 min to ensure adsorption–desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and TC molecules. Samples were taken every 20 min from the treated solution, and analyzed at 357 nm using an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer to determine the concentration of TC. After the photocatalytic reaction, CUP microspheres were recovered by centrifugation, thoroughly cleaned with deionized water to remove any residual contaminants, and dried for reuse in subsequent experiments.

In Fig. 1b, a compendium of TEM images is meticulously presented, delineating the structural attributes of UiO-66-NH2 and its amalgamation with CQDs, termed as CQD@UiO-66. The upper echelon of the panel unveils the unadulterated UiO-66-NH2 MOF, wherein the low magnification TEM portrayal unveils its quintessential, irregular polyhedral form, emblematic of its crystalline essence. A closer inspection affords a glimpse into the smooth and homogenous surface texturing, emblematic of the UiO-66-NH2 particles' well-orchestrated geometric constitution. In stark contrast, the lower tier of the panel introduces the CQD@UiO-66 nanocomposite. Here, the low magnification TEM image casts a noticeably darker contrast, a testament to the CQDs' incorporation within the MOF matrix. Elevated magnification TEM imaging acutely delineates the isolated CQDs, as demarcated by the yellow dotted peripheries, uniformly scattered over the surface of the UiO-66-NH2 entities. This composite genesis transcends mere physical amalgamation, intimating at a potential symbiotic interaction between the CQDs and the MOF scaffold. Such a confluence is anticipated to amplify the physicochemical characteristics significantly, broadening the scope of this novel composite's applicability across domains from photocatalysis to optoelectronics.

Fig. 1c elucidates the Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra pertaining to CQDs, UiO-66-NH2, and the CQD@UiO-66-NH2 composite. Within the spectral domain of the CQDs, a salient peak at 2858 cm−1 emerges, signifying the C–H stretching vibrations intrinsic to its molecular framework. Conversely, the UiO-66-NH2 spectrum manifests a pronounced peak at 867 cm−1, attributable to the bending vibrations inherent to amine groups. The spectrum of the CQD@UiO-66-NH2 composite amalgamates these spectral signatures, thereby denoting the coexistence of functional groups from both CQDs and UiO-66-NH2, which is indicative of a successful synthesis of the composite material. Fig. 1d unveils the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns for both UiO-66-NH2 and the CQD@UiO-66-NH2 composite, juxtaposed against a simulated pattern of UiO-66-NH2 for comparative analysis. The peaks located at 7.3, 8.4, and 25.6°, corresponding respectively to the (111), (002), and (006) planes, are discernible. The XRD pattern of UiO-66-NH2 is characterized by sharp, well-defined diffraction peaks, corroborating its crystalline constitution. In the CQD@UiO-66-NH2 pattern, while retaining the characteristic peaks of UiO-66-NH2, additional features are observed, potentially attributable to the incorporation of CQDs. This suggests the formation of a composite material that does not substantially disrupt the MOF's crystal structure. The alignment between the simulated and experimental XRD patterns of UiO-66-NH2 affirms the structural fidelity of the synthesized materials. Crucially, the integration of CQDs appears to uphold the crystalline integrity of the UiO-66-NH2 framework, a vital attribute for the efficacy of the composite in envisaged applications.

Fig. 1e elucidates the morphological attributes and elemental constitution of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles through SEM and EDS analysis. The SEM imagery reveals the nanoparticles' pronounced polyhedral form and their propensity for agglomeration. Adjacently, the EDS composite overlay image exhibits a uniform elemental spread, corroborating the SEM observations. This is further substantiated by individual EDS mappings, which vividly display the distribution of carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), and zirconium (Zr) within the nanoparticles, represented in hues of red, yellow, green, and cyan respectively. The consistent vibrancy of these colors throughout the EDS mappings indicates a harmonious integration of CQDs within the UiO-66-NH2 framework, thereby validating the successful creation of a well-composed nanocomposite. This uniformity in the composite structure is critical, as it assures a consistent performance of the material, rendering it highly suitable for avant-garde applications spanning catalysis, environmental rectification, and sensor technology.

Fig. 1f meticulously presents the X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of UiO-66-NH2, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and their hybrid CQD@UiO-66-NH2 composite, aiming to delineate their respective chemical states. The survey spectra authenticate the existence of fundamental elements, discernible through characteristic peaks attributed to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Zr 3d. In the high-resolution C 1s spectra of UiO-66-NH2, a deconvolution process reveals peaks pertaining to diverse carbon environments: C–H/C–C (284.3 eV), C-N (285.2 eV), and O-CO (288.4 eV). The CQD spectrum distinguishes itself with a unique C-O peak (286.2 eV), in addition to the anticipated C–H/C–C and O-CO functionalities. The composite material's C 1s spectrum amalgamates these spectral traits, signifying the integration of CQDs within the MOF matrix, as evidenced by the specific C-N and C-O binding energies. The O 1s high-resolution spectra further validate the composite's chemical makeup, with UiO-66-NH2 showing peaks linked to metal–oxygen bonds and assorted oxygenated functionalities, while the CQDs display a prominent peak denoting oxygen functionalities. The composite material's O 1s spectrum exhibits a blend of these oxygen states, enriched by additional peaks that underscore the interaction between CQDs and the UiO-66-NH2 framework. This XPS data collectively affirms the successful fabrication of the CQD@UiO-66-NH2 composite, with the chemical states of C and O providing insights into the interplay among the constituents. Such in-depth chemical profiling is crucial for anticipating and customizing the material's properties for potential utilizations in catalysis, adsorption, and sensor technologies, where specific molecular-level interactions critically influence the overall performance.

Fig. S1 presents the Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of UiO-66-NH2 and its CQD-functionalized derivative, CQD@UiO-66-NH2. The pristine UiO-66-NH2 material exhibits a pronounced PL peak at 443.6 nm, reflective of its luminescent attributes. With the incorporation of CQDs, the CQD@UiO-66-NH2 composite demonstrates an enhanced PL intensity at this wavelength, implying an elevated emission possibly due to the quantum confinement effect inherent in CQDs or improved energy transfer within the composite matrix. Fig. S1b and c delve into the luminescent traits of CQD@UiO-66-NH2, exploring the impact of concentration on PL intensity. The spectral analysis uncovers a concentration-dependent quenching effect, where higher concentrations of the composite precipitate a gradual reduction in emission. This quenching phenomenon is visually depicted in Fig. 1d, wherein under UV illumination, the luminescence visibly attenuates with increasing CQD@UiO-66-NH2 concentration. The most potent fluorescence is observed at the lowest concentration (0.001 g/L), with the intensity gradually increasing up to the highest concentration assessed (0.1 g/L). These observations are vital for applications reliant on regulated fluorescence, such as in the design of sensors or bioimaging tools, where concentration modulation can be strategically employed to achieve the desired luminescent intensity.

The synthesis of CUP porous microspheres, executed via the Pickering emulsion template method, capitalizes on the distinctive properties of UiO-66-NH2 and polysulfone (PSF) materials. This process commences with the formation of a homogenized solution of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 and PSF within the droplet, achieved through solvent exchange and evaporation techniques, followed by a phase separation phenomenon. As water migrates and coalesces within the droplet, it occupies the internal volume, engendering the emergence of a continuous liquid core within the microsphere [43]. Concomitant with the evaporation of dichloromethane (DCM), PSF precipitates, culminating in the creation of a hollow microsphere structure.

Fig. 1a presents a sequence of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images, which vividly depict the surface morphology of PSF microspheres, denoted as CUP-x, where 'x' represents the varying concentration gradient of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 utilized as a stabilizing agent within the Pickering emulsion framework. The portrayal extends from a control sample (Ctrl), devoid of CQD@UiO-66-NH2, to progressively increasing concentrations of the composite, ranging from CUP-0.08 to CUP-1.0. The control sample exhibits a relatively unblemished surface, intermittently punctuated with sporadic pores. As the concentration escalates from CUP-0.08 to CUP-1.0, there is a discernible shift towards enhanced surface roughness and porosity, marked by the incremental accretion of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles. Notably, at intermediary concentrations (CUP-0.4 to CUP-0.88), the microspheres demonstrate a pronounced increase in surface ruggedness and nanoparticle adhesion, reaching a zenith of densely clustered nanoparticle layers at CUP-0.88, as discernible in the high-magnification inset. The CUP-1.0 microspheres portray a thoroughly enshrouded and textured surface, indicative of peak nanoparticle saturation. These microspheres, with their meticulously adjustable interfacial properties, are poised for diverse applications spanning catalysis, separation technologies, drug delivery systems, and as specialized fillers in composite material production [42]. The depicted morphological progression, contingent upon nanoparticle concentration, is instrumental in linking the surface attributes of the microspheres to their functional performance and efficiency in various prospective applications.

The assessment of the mechanical robustness and stability of CUP microspheres is imperative, particularly in the context of their prospective deployment in photocatalytic applications. The absence of fractured microspheres within the analyzed sample serves as a testament to their substantial mechanical integrity and stability, rendering them apt for pragmatic utilization. To further substantiate the hollow architecture of the fabricated microspheres, a ball milling technique was employed to fragment the CUP microspheres, thereby yielding hemispherical sections. Fig. 2 b and c collectively offer an intricate morphological exposition of the CUP porous microspheres. The elemental analysis, as exhibited in the EDS of CUP-0.24 (Fig. S2), confirms a uniform elemental dispersion across the microsphere, a trait fundamentally pivotal to the material's photocatalytic efficacy. The SEM image in Fig. 2b vividly illustrates the hemispherical cross-section of a microsphere, unravelling a multitude of pores permeating its structure. This porosity is indicative of a substantial internal surface area, which is advantageous for facilitating enhanced mass transfer. The hollow nature of the microsphere significantly contributes to its photocatalytic prowess, as it enables efficacious mobility of reactants and products within the sphere, while concurrently offering an expansive surface area for the adsorption of pollutants. Moreover, the dense film of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles ensconcing the surface of the PSF microsphere augments photocatalytic activity by amplifying the surface area accessible for pollutant adsorption, thus elevating the efficiency of photocatalytic reactions. Complementing these observations, Fig. 2c presents a schematic representation that graphically delineates the microsphere's porosity and the strategic placement of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles upon its facade. This schematic highlights the uniformity in the attachment of nanoparticles, accentuating the meticulous design and structural coherence of these microspheres. Such detailed insight into the microspheres' morphology and composition is crucial for understanding and optimizing their function in various photocatalytic scenarios.

Fig. 3 a adeptly delineates the correlation between porosity and density in the CUP-x microspheres, encompassing varied concentrations of CQD@UiO-66-NH2. The bar graph elucidates a discernible trend where an escalation in the concentration of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 correlates with an increase in porosity. This suggests that the incorporation of these nanoparticles plays a pivotal role in fostering more porous structures within the microspheres. Conversely, an inverse relationship is observed with density, which diminishes as the CQD@UiO-66-NH2 content amplifies, implying that the composite microspheres become progressively lighter with higher nanoparticle concentrations. Specifically, as the CQD@UiO-66-NH2 content (x) ascends from 0.08 to 0.88, the porosity of the CUP-x samples escalates from 31.32 % to 45.26 %, while the density increases from 0.62 g/cm3 to 0.92 g/cm3. This observed trend can be ascribed to the addition of the higher-porosity CQD@UiO-66-NH2, which mitigates the porosity reduction typically engendered by phase separation in CUP microspheres. Moreover, alterations in the CUP microspheres' density are attributable to variations in sample porosity and the CQD@UiO-66-NH2 content. Given that the density of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 surpasses that of PSF, this accounts for the observed increase in the density of the CUP composite microspheres with the augmented addition of CQD@UiO-66-NH2. Experimentally, these microspheres exhibit excellent buoyancy in water, attributable to their low densities (for example, the density of cup-0.88 approximates 0.92 g/cm3) and their relatively high electrostatic repulsion (as shown in Fig. S3).

Fig. 3b presents the Fourier-Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra for PSF, CQD@UiO-66-NH2, and CUP-x microspheres with varying CQD@UiO-66-NH2 contents. These spectra reveal distinct absorption bands corresponding to the functional groups within the materials. Shifts and intensity modifications in these bands reflect compositional variances among the samples. Notably, the peak at 1657 cm−1 in the CQD@UiO-66-NH2 spectrum is indicative of carboxyl groups. Pronounced asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the OC-O group are observed at 1585 cm−1 and 1395 cm−1, respectively. Peaks at 771 and 664 cm−1 are attributed to the NH2-BDC ligand's O–H and C–H vibrations [44]. Additionally, the energy band near 1100 cm−1 signifies the stretching vibration of the metal–organic framework's Zr-O bonds [45]. These absorption bands, characterizing the respective functional groups, and their shifts and intensity variations offer profound insights into the compositional disparities among the samples.

The crystalline architecture of CUP microspheres was meticulously examined through XRD analysis, as depicted in Fig. 3c. The XRD pattern of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 exhibited four distinct diffraction peaks at 7.26°, 8.37°, 11.80°, 25.60°, and 30.54°, corresponding to the (111), (002), (022), (224), and (137) planes, respectively. These characteristic peaks are in alignment with those previously reported in literature for NH2-UiO-66(Zr) [46,47]. The XRD pattern of CUP-0.88 encompasses peaks from both CQD@UiO-66-NH2 and PSF, indicating the successful incorporation of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 into the PSF matrix. Results affirm that the growth on CUP does not compromise the crystallinity of CQD@UiO-66-NH2. Notably, the peak intensities in the XRD pattern of CUP-x mirror those of CQD@UiO-66-NH2, suggesting that the crystal structure of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 remains intact throughout the synthesis process.

Fig. 3d presents a log differential intrusion plot derived from mercury intrusion porosimetry, elucidating the pore size distribution within the CUP-x composite microspheres. The plot reveals a predominant pore size, indicated by a sharp peak, signifying a focused range of pore dimensions. This plot, alongside a schematic inset, visually depicts the mercury penetration within the pores, substantiating the quantitative analysis. The material exhibits a significant total intrusion volume of 1.24 mL/g and a considerable total pore area of 44.98 m2/g, indicative of a highly porous structure. The median pore diameters, calculated by volume and area, are 18,954.86 nm and 12.27 nm, respectively, denoting the central tendency in pore size distribution. An average pore diameter of 111.07 nm is reported, while the bulk and apparent densities are measured at 0.45 g/mL and 1.04 g/mL, respectively, denoting the material's mass relative to its volume, both excluding and including pore spaces. Remarkably, the porosity is quantified at an impressive 56.61 %, underscoring the material's substantial void fraction. These attributes are crucial for applications where porosity and surface area are critical to functionality, including adsorption, catalysis, and controlled release systems. In these applications, the precise internal architecture significantly influences performance efficacy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption and photocatalytic degradation performance of CUP on MB
ce:section.ce:para: The efficacy of CUP composite microspheres in the removal of MB was rigorously assessed through a series of dark adsorption and photocatalytic degradation experiments. The adsorption trials sought to establish the adsorption–desorption equilibrium of MB on various materials, including CUP, PSF, UiO-66-NH2/PSF, and CQD@UiO-66-NH2, by immersing them in an MB solution for 30 min. The initial MB concentration was maintained at 10 mg/L. Fig. 4 delineates the results of these dark adsorption experiments for CUP, CQD@UiO-66-NH2, and PSF. As evident from Fig. 4a, the adsorption capacity of both CUP and PSF microspheres exhibits a rapid initial increase, subsequently plateauing until equilibrium is attained. The porous nature of the CUP microspheres, along with the presence of CQD@UiO-66-NH2, amplifies the surface area, thereby augmenting the number of potential adsorption sites for MB+ ions. Furthermore, the electrostatic attraction between the positively charged MB+ ions and the negatively charged PSF material significantly bolsters the adsorption process, resulting in a markedly higher rate compared to PSF microspheres in isolation. The electrostatic adsorption facilitated by negatively charged PSF materials, although weaker than that of porous materials like CQD@UiO-66-NH2, indicates the superior adsorptive capabilities of CUP towards dye molecules. This enhanced adsorption can be attributed to the increased surface area provided by the porous structures inherent in both CQD@UiO-66-NH2 and its associated CUP particles, coupled with the presence of active sites that efficaciously attract dye molecules.

Subsequently, the pseudo-first-order model, pseudo-second-order model, and intraparticle diffusion model were used to analyze the adsorption performance of the adsorbent on dye molecules (Fig. 4b-d). The calculation formula is as follows [48]:

where, qe (g mg−1) is the adsorption equilibrium amount, qt is the adsorption amount at time t, k1 (min−1) is the pseudo-first-order curve rate constant, k2 (g·mg−1·min−1) is the quasi-second-order curve rate constant, and k3(g·mg−1·min−0.5) is the rate constant of particle internal diffusion coefficient. Fig. 4b elucidates the adsorption kinetics of the microspheres using a pseudo-first-order model. This model posits an initially rapid adsorption phase, predominantly due to the abundance of unoccupied sites, which progressively decelerates as these sites are gradually saturated. The linearity observed in the initial phase of the adsorption curves corroborates the suitability of this kinetic model. Conversely, Fig. 4c adopts a pseudo-second-order model, postulating that the adsorption process aligns more closely with chemisorption kinetics. In this scenario, the adsorption rate is proportionate to the square of the availability of adsorption sites, suggesting potential mechanisms involving electron sharing or exchange. The linear progression of the t/qt versus time graph underscores the predominance of this model in explicating the adsorption kinetics under specific conditions. Fig. 4d delves into the intraparticle diffusion model, where the multilinear trajectory of the plots indicates that adsorption onto CUP-x microspheres is governed by multiple steps. The initial sharp incline in each plot signifies surface adsorption, which then transitions to a plateau phase where intraparticle diffusion emerges as the rate-limiting factor. In comparison to individual PSF, UiO-66-NH2/PSF, and CQD@UiO-66-NH2, the CUP composite microspheres demonstrate enhanced proficiency in the removal of methylene blue (MB). The adsorption behavior of CUP, CQD@UiO-66-NH2, and PSF is best delineated by a pseudo-first-order model, as depicted in Fig. 4b. This indicates that the adsorption rate is primarily governed by the availability of active sites on the microspheres' surface [49]. Moreover, the roughened surface of the composite microspheres furnishes a greater number of adsorption sites compared to the smoother surface of PSF, while the porous structure of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 further facilitates dye molecule adsorption. An analysis based on the particle internal diffusion model also reveals that the diffusion of MB within the CUP composite microspheres is influenced by both the presence of pores on the sphere surfaces and the unique mass transfer dynamics within these pores. This underscores the pivotal role of the porous structure and roughened surface in enhancing the adsorption performance of the composite spheres.

The data presented in Table 1 underscores the diverse specific surface areas and pore structures of the materials under study, which are instrumental in understanding their varying adsorption capabilities. The notably higher surface area and smaller pore size of UiO-66-NH2 suggest a greater potential for adsorption compared to CQD@UiO-66-NH2, which has a reduced surface area possibly due to CQDs occupying pore spaces. The considerable pore volume of PSF indicates its capacity for bulk adsorption despite a lower surface area, while the integration of UiO-66-NH2 into PSF (UiO-66-NH2/PSF) moderately enhances its surface area and reduces pore size, potentially improving adsorption efficiency. The specific surface area and pore structure of CUP are distinct from the individual components, which may result from the composite formation and affect its adsorption performance.

Fig. 4e and f in the manuscript meticulously delineate the adsorptive and photocatalytic degradation capabilities of various composites and materials, with MB serving as the target pollutant. Fig. 4e illustrates the normalized concentration of MB (C/C0) over time, manifesting the augmented degradation efficiency of the CUP-x composites compared to their individual constituents, UiO-66-NH2 and PSF. The observed decline in C/C0 for each composite underscores the effectiveness of both adsorption and catalysis processes, with the trend suggesting that the integration of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 bolsters photocatalytic activity, particularly at higher loadings. Fig. 4f offers insights into the kinetics of the photocatalytic process by charting the natural logarithm of the MB concentration ratio ln(C/C0) against time. The slopes of these lines, representing the rate constants of the photo-Fenton-like reaction, reveal more precipitous degradation of MB, with steeper slopes indicating faster reaction rates. This kinetic analysis emphasizes the enhanced reaction velocities for the CUP-x composites compared to the base materials, highlighting the efficacy of nanoparticle augmentation in photocatalytic efficiency. Collectively, these results underscore the potential of CUP-x composites in environmental remediation applications, particularly in the photocatalytic treatment of wastewater. Optimizing the CQD@UiO-66-NH2 content emerges as a critical factor for maximizing degradation efficacy.

An investigation into the recyclability and degradation performance of MB after the recovery of CUP-0.88 was conducted. The CUP-0.88 catalyst, post-degradation, was extensively washed with deionized water, centrifuged, and then reused in subsequent adsorption and degradation experiments. Fig. 4g-i provide a comprehensive analysis of the recyclability and structural resilience of CUP-0.88 microspheres in photocatalytic degradation of MB. Fig. 4g delineates the retention of photocatalytic efficiency across five consecutive cycles, with only a slight decrease in degradation rates, as indicated by the C/C0 ratios over time. The inset recovery rates in Fig. 4g exhibit a high level of microsphere recuperation after each cycle, attesting to the durability of the photocatalytic activity. This is corroborated by the SEM image, which visually affirms the microspheres' structural integrity following repeated uses. The photocatalytic activity of CUP-0.88 maintains a degradation rate of 91.32 % without stirring after 5 cycles, highlighting the effective photocatalytic degradation and recyclability of CUP composite microspheres for MB organic dyes. Fig. 4h contrasts the dark adsorption and photocatalytic degradation efficiencies over the cycles, indicating a more pronounced decline in photocatalytic performance, possibly due to the progressive inactivation of catalytic sites [50]. Lastly, Fig. 4i exhibits the XRD patterns pre- and post-recycling process, revealing sustained crystallinity with no significant structural alteration. This is further validated in the FT-IR spectrum of the recycled CUP porous microcapsules (Fig. S4). Together, these figures affirm the CUP-0.88 microspheres as a robust photocatalyst, maintaining considerable activity and structural soundness even after multiple reuse cycles, which is crucial for their application in sustainable water treatment and environmental remediation strategies.

Fig. S5 delineates the photodegradation proficiency of MB across quintuple cycles employing CUP-0.88 and CQD@UiO-66-NH2, alongside their respective recuperation rates post each cycle. In a, both entities exhibit a diminution in degradation efficiency through successive cycles, with CUP-0.88 preserving a superior rate continuously. Fig. S5b portrays the recuperation rates of both materials, revealing that CUP-0.88 maintains a relatively stable recovery rate, in contrast to CQD@UiO-66, which demonstrates a more pronounced decline. This suggests a potential diminishment in activity or structural integrity with recurrent cycles. In the cyclic stability assay, CUP manifested a recovery rate exceeding 82 %, whereas the recovery rate of CQD@UiO-66 diminished to 57 % after five cycles. Concurrently, CUP upheld a cyclic degradation efficiency surpassing 93.3 % after five cycles, while the efficiency of CQD@UiO-66 plummeted to 53.64 %. The PSF microspheres potentially confer a protective effect during the cyclic process, forestalling structural alterations in CUP. Its lesser density and porous architecture may function as a safeguard in composite materials, decelerating the material's degradation trajectory. Moreover, during the cyclic degradation sequence, active constituents (such as CQD@UiO-66) may gradually deplete due to repetitive utilization, culminating in a diminished activity of CQD@UiO-66. In this scenario, the relative constancy of CUP assumes paramount significance. The adsorptive properties of PSF are anticipated to augment the stability of CUP. Additionally, PSF's adsorption capabilities may facilitate the sequestration and immobilization of deleterious substances liberated in circulation, thereby sustaining CUP's performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation performance of tetracycline by CUP porous microcapsules
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of CUP microspheres was evaluated using tetracycline (TC) as a representative pollutant. Fig. 5 a illustrates that in the absence of a photocatalyst, the concentration of TC diminishes only slightly, attributable to direct photolysis by simulated sunlight. However, the introduction of a photocatalyst markedly enhances TC removal. Owing to the porous structure of CQD@UiO-66-NH2, it facilitates the absorption of approximately 18 % of TC prior to degradation. The incorporation of CQD significantly boosts the final TC removal efficiency of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 to 100 %, which is 1.4 times higher than that of UiO-66-NH2. The optimal removal rate achieved is 0.05 mg·L−1·min−1. Fig. 5b presents TC degradation kinetics, which adhere to a pseudo-first-order reaction pattern, indicative of typical heterogeneous catalysis [51]. The relationship between ln(C0/Ct) and time underscores that CQD@UiO-66-NH2 substantially accelerates TC degradation. The reaction rate constant (k) for CQD@UiO-66-NH2 is 0.02768 min−1, in contrast to 0.00672 min−1 for original UiO-66 and 0.00351 min−1 for direct photolysis. The CQD-enhanced UiO-66-NH2 exhibits superior catalytic efficiency in decomposing TC molecules.

Fig. 5c examines the light absorption behavior of CUP through its ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectrum in the 200–800 nm wavelength range. The results reveal that CQD possesses a broad absorption band in the ultraviolet–visible light spectrum, with a maximum absorption peak at approximately 443.6 nm. The absorption peak of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 at 443.6 nm is notably intensified by CQD, indicating that CQD@UiO-66-NH2 and PSF effectively combine to form a new material with enhanced visible light absorption. This introduces an appropriate band gap, significantly enhancing the visible light responsiveness of photo-Fenton catalysts [51], thereby improving the photocatalytic activity of CUP composite microspheres.

Fig. 5d displays the transient photocurrent response of CUP composites with different CQD concentrations under visible light irradiation. The addition of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 to CUP-0.88 helps in preventing the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thus augmenting its photocurrent. The photocurrent intensity, observed over time, shows periodic peaks corresponding to the light source's on/off cycles. Various curves represent composites with 0.2 wt%, 0.5 wt%, and 1.0 wt% CQD, suggesting that increased CQD content enhances photocurrent generation, thereby implying elevated photoactivity, advantageous for both photovoltaic and photocatalytic applications [52]. Moreover, the interfacial impedance between CUP-0.88 and the electrolyte solution was examined using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The Nyquist plot of CUP-0.88 (Fig. S6) exhibits a small semicircle radius, indicative of a high charge transfer capacity at the interface [53]. TPC and EIS analyses demonstrate that CUP-0.88 promotes the separation of electron-hole pairs. In addition, CUP achieves a degradation efficiency of 100 % with a rate constant of 0.02768 min−1 at a catalyst dosage of 0.5 g/L (as listed in Table 2 ), thereby establishing itself as a highly effective photocatalyst compared to others in the table. Fig. S7 confirms the effective mineralization capabilities of CUP-0.88, where a marked reduction in TOC and COD levels over a 24-hour period demonstrates its potential for efficient organic pollutant degradation and water purification. Moreover, the quantitative structure–activity analysis presented in Table S1 demonstrates that strategic modifications in surface area, pore volume, and particle size significantly enhance the catalytic activity of the CUP-0.88, substantiating the efficacy of our targeted catalyst design. These findings highlight the CQD@UiO-66-NH₂ composite as a promising candidate for efficient photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline in aqueous solutions.

In the realm of photocatalysis, superoxide (
O2 –) and hydroxyl (
OH) radicals are recognized as integral reactive species [61]. To authenticate their presence, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was employed, utilizing 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trap. The resultant ESR spectra, as delineated in Fig. 5h and i, offer a profound insight into this phenomenon. Fig. 5e and f present the ESR spectra of DMPO adducts in the milieu of CUP composites, thereby illustrating the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) during the photocatalytic process. In Fig. 5e, the ESR spectra pertaining to DMPO-
O2 – (superoxide) radical adducts display a quintessential quartet signal spanning all samples, encompassing both pure UiO-66/PSF and its composites with varying CQD concentrations (0.2 wt%, 0.5 wt%, and 1.0 wt%). The fluctuation in signal intensity, contingent on the CQD content, intimates that the quantum of CQDs modulates either the production or stabilization of superoxide radicals. This suggests a nuanced interplay between the CQD concentration and the generation of these radicals. Concurrently, Fig. 5f elucidates the spectra for DMPO-
OH (hydroxyl) radical adducts, where the distinctive quartet of peaks signifies the presence of hydroxyl radicals. The variances in peak intensities across diverse samples insinuate the influence of CQDs on the genesis or sequestration of hydroxyl radicals. These ESR revelations are paramount in demystifying the photocatalytic mechanism of CUP composites. They illuminate how the incorporation level of CQDs exerts a direct impact on the formation of ROS, which are instrumental in the degradation of organic contaminants. Photocatalytic processes engender radical species such as
OH and
O2 – radicals, alongside singlet oxygen (1O2) [62]. The catalytic prowess of our composite, CUP, in degrading MB was assessed through radical scavenging experiments, illuminated in Fig. S8. Quenchers like L-histidine, TBA, and BQ targeted 1O2,
OH, and
O2 – radicals respectively, elucidating their integral role in MB degradation [63]. The quenchers' introduction corresponded with a marked diminution in degradation efficacy—23.8 %, 13.1 %, and 36.6 % respectively—underscoring the predominant role of
O2 – radicals and 1O2 in the process.

We explicate the underlying mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of aqueous contaminants employing an innovative composite nanoparticle system (see Fig. 6 ), comprising CQDs amalgamated with the metal–organic framework UiO-66-NH2. Upon exposure to both visible (λ1) and ultraviolet (λ2) light, these composite materials manifest a synergistic photoreaction. Herein, CQDs function as visible light harvesters, generating electron-hole pairs, whilst UiO-66-NH2 is concurrently activated by UV illumination. Electrons in the conduction band of UiO-66-NH2 (−0.83 eV) exhibit ample reductive potential to transform molecular oxygen into superoxide radicals (
O2 –), and conversely, the residual holes in the valence band of CQDs (+1.78 eV) act to oxidize water molecules, engendering hydroxyl radicals (
OH). These reactive oxygen species play a pivotal role in the degradation process, efficiently mineralizing contaminants into innocuous byproducts. The photogenerated charge segregation and transference are energetically optimized, courtesy of the respective band gaps of UiO-66-NH2 (approximately 2.90 eV) and CQDs (approximately 2.28 eV), congruent with the photon energies, thus augmenting the photocatalytic operation. This composite photocatalytic system demonstrates substantial potential in the realm of environmental remediation.

This study marks a significant advancement in the realm of photocatalytic materials through the successful synthesis of MOF@Polysulfone porous microspheres, enhanced with CQDs, using a Pickering emulsion template method. The composite material showcased remarkable photocatalytic degradation capabilities, particularly against tetracycline, a widespread antibiotic contaminant. The synthesis process created a covalent bond between the amino group of UiO-66-NH2 and the carboxyl group of CQD, resulting in a composite with a markedly increased specific surface area and numerous active sites. These attributes are vital for its enhanced photocatalytic efficiency and recyclability. The surface properties of the composite, especially its porosity, played a pivotal role in its effectiveness. The dense nanoparticle film of CQD@UiO-66-NH2 on the PSF microsphere surface increased the area available for pollutant adsorption, thereby boosting photocatalytic reactions. Porosity measurements showed an increase proportional to the concentration of CQD@UiO-66-NH2, suggesting the formation of more porous structures upon nanoparticle incorporation. In terms of stability and recyclability, CUP-0.88 microspheres exhibited outstanding structural durability and consistent photocatalytic performance over several cycles, making them viable for practical applications. The composite’s photocatalytic proficiency was particularly remarkable, achieving a 100 % degradation efficiency of tetracycline with a rate constant of 0.02768 min−1 at a catalyst dosage of 0.5 g/L. This performance underscores its superiority over other photocatalysts. Spectral analysis of DMPO-
OH radical adducts shed light on the mechanistic aspects, highlighting CQDs' role in generating or capturing hydroxyl radicals, thus elucidating the photocatalytic mechanism of the CUP composites. Furthermore, the high mechanical strength and stability of the CUP microspheres were confirmed by the absence of any broken microspheres in post-experimental samples. The hollow structure, as revealed by SEM imaging, facilitated the efficient movement of reactants and products within the microsphere and provided an extensive surface area for pollutant adsorption, significantly enhancing photocatalytic activity. The findings of this study make a substantial contribution to environmental remediation efforts, particularly in developing effective water purification and organic pollutant degradation techniques, and underscore the potential of advanced photocatalytic materials.

Yannan Zhang: Visualization, Methodology. Baoting Dong: Visualization. Zhuo Li: Methodology. Wenjun Qu: Methodology, Data curation. Chao Liu: Visualization. Zhaoping Song: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Peng Lu: Resources, Funding acquisition. Dandan Ji: Resources. Wenxia Liu: Supervision.
